topic: kick bench smoke 2-line haiku poetic devices
purpose: Bench-smoke the kick(ω-cycle) kernel against a non-software literary topic — poetic devices admissible inside a 2-line haiku — to verify the 5-op kernel (intake → pre-register falsifiers → fan-out K≥3 orthogonal axes → falsifier-pass-pattern gate → witness emit) holds when domain is poetics rather than code. The witness IS the smoke evidence: kernel correctness is judged by whether (a) K≥3 orthogonal axes are surfaced despite only 2 declared, (b) Tier-1 candidates land via falsifier-pass-pattern, (c) α/β/γ envelopes propagate verbatim, (d) drill stratum (multi-round refinement) produces a coherent witness without regressing to single-shot smash.

abstraction_stage.domain_relevance: absorbed-primitive seed steers axis selection toward devices that survived prior absorbsmoke synthesis (auto-absorb closed loop)

abstraction_stage.domain_relevance: qrng_byte=51 deterministically picked the 2-line constraint reframe over the canonical 5-7-5 — entropy bit forces non-default form

abstraction_stage.domain_relevance: gaia_bridge biases imagery axis toward earth/seasonal kigo — relevant to haiku kigo enumeration; OFFLINE-FALLBACK acknowledged

pre_registered_falsifiers.evidence: all cited devices (kireji, kigo, ma, juxtaposition, alliteration, assonance, consonance) are standard poetic-tradition terminology — no invented schools or invented author names

axes_surfaced.name: Form compression: 5-7-5 mora budget reorganized into 2-line structure
options_considered: A. truncate to first 2 lines of 5-7-5 (5+7=12 mora, kireji forced at end of line 2) — rejected: drops final 5 mora and the resolution image
B. fuse lines 1+2 (5+7=12) and use line 3 alone (5) — rejected: imbalanced 12+5 violates haiku breath
C. redistribute 17 mora as 8+9 or 9+8 with kireji at internal cut (chosen) — preserves total mora load and the kireji function
D. abandon mora count; preserve only kireji+kigo as 2-line minimum (rejected: drifts into senryū territory)
axes_surfaced.chosen: C. redistribute 17 mora as 8+9 or 9+8 with internal kireji cut
axes_surfaced.rationale: Preserves total syllabic budget and the structural cut; only the line-count surface changes. This option is the only one that retains all three canonical haiku elements (kireji + kigo + juxtaposition) under a 2-line constraint.
axes_surfaced.measurement_surface: structural/formal (mora count, line break placement, kireji position)

axes_surfaced.name: Falsifier coverage: what disqualifies a candidate as a 2-line haiku
options_considered: A. count-only falsifier: reject if mora ≠ 17 (too narrow — admits prose-disguised entries)
B. function falsifier: reject if no kireji-induced cut detectable (chosen, primary) — captures the structural soul
C. seasonality falsifier: reject if no kigo (chosen, secondary) — kigo is constitutive of haiku vs senryū
D. imagery-not-statement falsifier: reject if line 1 OR 2 is abstract proposition (chosen, tertiary)
axes_surfaced.rationale: Single falsifier misses the form; three orthogonal gates (cut + season + image) capture the device-density a 2-line haiku must hit to compensate for the lost line.

axes_surfaced.name: Phonological density: per-line sonic-device load when the line budget shrinks
options_considered: A. preserve 3-line phonemic density unchanged (rejected: 2-line form has 33% less surface — same density yields fewer total devices)
B. raise per-line density via alliteration + internal assonance to match 3-line total device count (chosen)
C. swap phonemic devices for visual devices like kanji-shape pairing (rejected: visual is a separate channel, not a phonemic substitute)
axes_surfaced.rationale: If 2-line form must carry the same sonic weight as 3-line, devices must densify per line. Alliteration on the kireji-cut consonants and internal assonance across the cut are the cheapest densifiers.
axes_surfaced.measurement_surface: phonemic/auditory (alliteration count, assonance count, consonance count per line)

axes_surfaced.name: Imagery juxtaposition: diptych vs single-image schema across the kireji cut
options_considered: A. single sustained image across both lines (rejected: collapses kireji function — no juxtaposition)
B. diptych: line 1 = image-A, kireji-cut, line 2 = image-B; juxtaposition emerges from collision (chosen)
C. nested images (image inside image) — rejected: too dense for 2-line economy
axes_surfaced.rationale: The kireji cut is the haiku's primary semantic engine. In 2-line form the cut becomes more salient (only one cut available), so the diptych schema is forced rather than chosen.

pairwise_correlation_matrix.verdict: PASS — all pairs below 0.70 orthogonality threshold

tier_1_implemented.rationale: In a 2-line haiku, kireji must land at the line break (only cut available). The cutting word's sole job is to force juxtaposition between line-A and line-B. Without it, the form degenerates to a couplet.
tier_1_implemented.example_marker: や、かな、けり — or English-language equivalents (em-dash, colon, line-break itself)

tier_1_implemented.device: kigo (季語) — seasonal reference compressed into a single image
tier_1_implemented.rationale: Without a kigo, the form is senryū not haiku. Under 2-line constraint the kigo must be folded into one of the two images (cannot afford a separate kigo line). Compression forces dual-purpose words: image + seasonal marker simultaneously.
tier_1_implemented.example_marker: cherry blossom (spring), cicada (summer), persimmon (autumn), bare branch (winter) — each carries season + image in one phrase

tier_1_implemented.device: alliteration + assonance + consonance — densified per line
tier_1_implemented.rationale: 2-line form has 33% less surface than 3-line. To preserve sonic weight, per-line phonemic device count must rise. Alliteration anchored at kireji and assonance straddling the cut are the most economical densifiers.
tier_1_implemented.example_marker: matched initial consonants line-1↔line-2; shared vowel across the cut

tier_1_implemented.device: diptych juxtaposition — two-panel image structure across the kireji
tier_1_implemented.rationale: Kireji-cut creates a visual diptych: image-A | image-B. Juxtaposition is the haiku's semantic engine; in 2-line form the diptych is forced (only one cut, so binary partition is canonical). Reader's mind supplies the third element via gestalt.
tier_1_implemented.example_marker: concrete-vs-abstract, near-vs-far, animate-vs-inanimate — any binary axis of contrast

tier_1_implemented.device: ma (間) — negative-space economy; what's omitted carries weight
tier_1_implemented.rationale: Lost line = lost stated content but gained ma. The unsaid becomes a load-bearing structural element — the absent third line is felt as silence/breath. This is uniquely 2-line-form-specific: not present in 3-line haiku because no line is missing.
tier_1_implemented.example_marker: the resolution image is implied not stated; reader completes the gestalt

drill_passes.action: enumerated candidate poetic devices applicable to haiku in general
drill_passes.drop_reason: not 2-line-form-specific; available in any short poetry — fails F6 specificity gate

drill_passes.action: computed pairwise correlation matrix; verified all <0.70 per raw 48

drill_passes.name: falsifier-rationale-tightening
drill_passes.action: rewrote each falsifier verdict_check to be string-checkable rather than narrative

no_fabrication_evidence: kireji (切れ字), kigo (季語), ma (間) — standard Japanese haiku-tradition vocabulary; verifiable in any haiku reference
alliteration, assonance, consonance, juxtaposition — standard prosody terminology

no specific haiku author quoted; no specific haiku text fabricated
